narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 46.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to raise concerns about your readiness. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your Year 1 school decile of 5 and Year 3 decile of 4—both below the typical student's 5.7 and 5.5 averages—along with your LSAT score of 33.5, which trails the average of 36.7, and your undergraduate GPA of 3.3, slightly above the norm of 3.2. Your middle family income bracket aligns with many applicants, you're a white male like most in the dataset, and opting for part-time status is less common than full-time. Among these, your Year 3 decile had the strongest influence, pushing the model toward predicting failure since it's lower than what succeeds for typical students. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile, similarly below average and reinforcing doubts about consistent performance. Your LSAT score followed closely, as lower scores like yours often correlate with bar struggles compared to peers. Family income and racial background played supporting roles in tipping toward failure, though less decisively. Your undergraduate GPA nudged slightly against failure, your gender helped a bit toward success, and part-time status had minimal sway. Overall, these early academic deciles and LSAT stood out as the top drivers, unlike stronger profiles where high deciles and LSATs dominate positive predictions. Compared to typical admitted students, your grades and test scores lag in ways that signal higher risk. To boost future chances, focus on retaking the LSAT for a higher score if possible, strengthening any upcoming grades to lift those deciles, and preparing rigorously for the bar through targeted study or prep courses. With improvements there, you could shift the model's view dramatically. Keep pushing—you have strengths like your solid GPA to build on.
